schemaNote: Masters' principles for voicing selection (#220). Each principle is paraphrased in the maintainer's own words. References cite sources for further study. voicingStyleTags / playStyleTags map to the Stage-1-reserved voicing schema fields; engine consumption follows in a Track-3 ticket. Initial principles drafted from general knowledge — the project owner will refine and expand.

masters.name: George Van Eps
masters.biography: Pioneer of the 7-string electric guitar with the added low A string. Developed a body of theory treating the guitar as a contrapuntal instrument: independent bass, inner voices, and melody moving simultaneously, often beneath sustained chord shapes.

principles.name: Comping with self
principles.summary: The 7th string (low A) carries an independent bass voice while the chord above it sustains. Bass moves tonic-dominant-tonic or chromatic neighbor while the upper four voices hold the chord; the listener hears two parts at once.

references.source: Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar (Mel Bay, 3 vols)
references.citation: Van Eps, George. Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar. Mel Bay, 1980-1982.

principles.name: Vertical structures
principles.summary: Chord-melody where the bass note is part of the unified vertical structure, not an isolated low note. Voicings are designed so every interval contributes to the chord's identity; no 'spare' notes added for body.

references.source: Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar Vol I
references.citation: Van Eps. HM Vol I, sections on triad-with-bass forms.

masters.name: Ted Greene
masters.biography: Los Angeles teacher and player whose lessons reshaped how guitarists think about chord voicings, voice-leading, and the interaction of melody with harmony. Authored Chord Chemistry and Modern Chord Progressions; his estate (tedgreene.com) maintains his manuscript and lesson archive openly.

principles.name: Voice leading through inner motion
principles.summary: Melody stays on top while inner voices (alto, tenor) move stepwise between chord changes. The bass anchors, the top voice carries the melody, and the inner voices do the harmonic work — producing the sense of multiple lines weaving rather than block chords thudding.

references.citation: Greene, Ted. Chord Chemistry. Dale Zdenek, 1971.

principles.name: Sequential articulation
principles.summary: Voicings are designed for hybrid-pick or fingerstyle plucking with a specific right-hand sequence: bass plucked first, then the inner voices in order, melody on top. The pluck order is part of the music; strumming the same shape sounds different.

references.citation: Greene, Ted. Modern Chord Progressions. Dale Zdenek, 1973.

masters.name: Joe Pass
masters.biography: Bebop guitarist whose solo recordings (Virtuoso series, 1973-1983) reset expectations for unaccompanied jazz guitar. Combined walking bass lines with chord stabs and single-note runs from held chord positions.

principles.name: Walking bass plus chord
principles.summary: Bass note on every beat with chord stabs on the off-beats. Requires voicings that don't bury the bass string and whose chord stab is short enough to leave room for the next bass note. Often 2-3 note chord stabs rather than full block voicings.

references.citation: Pass, Joe and Bill Thrasher. Joe Pass Guitar Style. Gwyn Publishing, 1971.

principles.name: Single-note runs from chord shapes
principles.summary: Improvise melodically by playing single notes drawn from the chord shape currently held. The held shape acts as a position anchor; the soloing fingers move between the chord tones without requiring a separate scale framework.

references.citation: Pass and Thrasher. Joe Pass Guitar Style. Gwyn, 1971.

masters.name: Martin Taylor
masters.biography: Scottish virtuoso whose solo fingerstyle approach extends the Joe Pass tradition: walking bass, chord stabs, and melody played simultaneously on a single guitar. Distinguished from Pass by all-fingers right-hand technique (no plectrum) and a more orchestral chord-voicing palette. Authored instructional series (Single Note Soloing, Solo Jazz Guitar Mastering) and operates an active online lesson archive.

principles.name: Thumb-bass independence
principles.summary: The right-hand thumb plays an independent bass line (often walking quarter notes) while the fingers handle chord stabs and melody. The bass is rhythmically and contrapuntally independent of the chord/melody, not just a low root.

references.source: Martin Taylor's Solo Jazz Guitar (Mel Bay)
references.citation: Taylor, Martin. Solo Jazz Guitar. Mel Bay.

principles.name: Orchestral voicing palette
principles.summary: Voicing choice spans more than block chords — at any moment the guitar may be sounding bass + chord, bass + counterline, double-stop + melody, or full block voicing. The 'right' voicing in a Taylor arrangement depends on which texture the moment calls for, not a single default.

masters.name: Wes Montgomery
masters.biography: Self-taught Indianapolis guitarist whose innovations (thumb-attack right hand, octave melodic statements, block-chord chorus climax) reshaped jazz guitar after 1959. Recordings are the primary documentation; written method books are mostly posthumous transcription collections.

principles.name: Three-tier chorus development
principles.summary: A solo arcs from single-note lines through octave statements to block-chord harmonization. Each tier carries melodic content; the chord tier voices the same melodic shape with full block voicings underneath. The block-chord tier is where chord-voicing choice matters most — those voicings are melody-led, not chord-led.

references.source: The Wes Montgomery Guitar Folio (Hal Leonard)
references.citation: Montgomery, Wes. The Wes Montgomery Guitar Folio. Hal Leonard. Posthumous transcriptions.

principles.name: Thumb-attack articulation
principles.summary: Right-hand technique uses the thumb only (no plectrum, no fingerstyle multi-finger). This constrains practical voicings: block chords with bass on the lower strings are thumb-friendly; sweeps across many strings are not. Voicings get chosen partly for executability under this constraint.

masters.name: Lenny Breau
masters.biography: Canadian guitarist who synthesized jazz harmony with classical fingerstyle technique. Known for playing three independent voices simultaneously and for cascading artificial-harmonic textures.

principles.name: Three independent voices on six strings
principles.summary: Bass, chord, and melody played simultaneously via fingerstyle. The chord voice is often only 2-3 notes (not a full block) to leave room for the bass below and melody above. Right hand: thumb on bass, fingers on chord and melody.

references.citation: Breau, Lenny. The Velvet Touch of Lenny Breau (RCA, 1969) — recorded reference.

principles.name: Cascading harmonics
principles.summary: Artificial harmonics intermixed with fretted notes for harp-like cascades. Right hand executes the harmonic with thumb/index touching 12 frets above the fretted position while picking with the ring finger.

masters.name: Jim Hall
masters.biography: Influential jazz comper and soloist whose voicings emphasize space, fourths, and conversational interaction with the soloist. Recordings with Bill Evans, Sonny Rollins, and Paul Desmond defined a quieter approach to jazz guitar accompaniment.

principles.name: Open voicings with fourths
principles.summary: Wide intervals between adjacent voices, often using stacked fourths instead of triadic thirds-and-fifths stacks. Produces a modern, ambiguous sound that doesn't commit to a single tonality.

references.citation: Hall, Jim. Exploring Jazz Guitar. Hal Leonard.

principles.name: Space in comping
principles.summary: Playing less, leaving room for the soloist or for melody to ring. Fewer notes per voicing (often 2-3); strategic silences. The voicing's job is to imply the chord, not exhaustively spell it.

masters.name: Johnny Smith
masters.biography: Quiet master of close-voiced chord-melody. His 1952 recording 'Moonlight in Vermont' became a template for the genre. Voicings are tight (often all within 4 frets), with the melody close above the chord rather than floating two octaves higher.

principles.name: Tight voicings
principles.summary: Closely-voiced chord-melody, often within 4 frets, every voice within an octave of the others. Produces a unified vertical sound rather than a wide-spread harp texture.

references.source: Mel Bay's Johnny Smith Approach to Guitar
references.citation: Smith, Johnny. Johnny Smith Approach to Guitar. Mel Bay.

principles.name: Single-string melody under chord
principles.summary: Melody dropped to lower strings while upper voices sustain a chord above it. Inverts the usual chord-melody direction; the chord becomes the sky and the melody walks the ground.

masters.name: Jody Fisher
masters.biography: Educator whose Alfred Music series (Jazz Guitar Method, Chord Tone Soloing, Mastering Jazz Guitar) systematized jazz voicing study for generations of self-taught players. Codified the drop-2 / drop-3 / spread voicing taxonomy as a pedagogical framework.

principles.name: Drop-voicing pedagogical taxonomy
principles.summary: Categorize voicings systematically by which voice is 'dropped' from a close-position stack: drop-2 (drop the second-from-top), drop-3 (drop the third-from-top), drop-2-and-4 (drop both). The taxonomy makes the otherwise-overwhelming voicing space teachable.

references.citation: Fisher, Jody. Complete Jazz Guitar Method. Alfred Music.

principles.name: Position-based practice methodology
principles.summary: Systematic chord-quality study through CAGED positions or position-fixed walks: take a position, cycle every chord quality through it, only THEN move position. Builds vocabulary in a position rather than overwhelming the learner with mobility immediately.

references.citation: Fisher, Jody. Mastering Jazz Guitar: Chord/Melody. Alfred Music.
